items.q: Can sucrose move in both directions in the phloem?
items.correct: Yes — translocation goes from any source (e.g. leaves) to any sink (e.g. roots, fruits), so direction varies seasonally and between phloem sieve tubes.
items.misconception: Phloem always carries sucrose downwards from leaves to roots.
items.random_wrong: Phloem carries water upwards and sucrose downwards in the same tube.
items.why: Students conflate phloem with xylem in terms of being unidirectional; they don't realise source-sink relationships can reverse (e.g. early spring, roots are sources).

items.q: What does negative feedback do in homeostasis?
items.correct: It reverses a change in a controlled variable, bringing it back towards the set point.
items.misconception: Negative feedback makes a process get worse or amplifies the change.
items.random_wrong: Negative feedback stops the body from responding to any internal change.
items.why: The word 'negative' has bad connotations, so students think it makes things worse, missing that it counteracts deviations.

items.correct: Each new DNA molecule contains one original (parental) strand and one newly synthesised strand.
items.misconception: One daughter molecule contains both original strands; the other contains both new strands.
items.random_wrong: DNA replication produces two identical fully new molecules with no original material.
items.why: Students confuse semi-conservative with conservative replication — they don't grasp that each daughter molecule is a hybrid of old and new strands, as Meselson-Stahl demonstrated.
Correct answer: Glycosidic. “Peptide” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Addition of water. “Loss of water” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
